@graph.name: 9 Materials That Help With Motor Planning
@graph.description: Some children understand exactly what to do but can't get their body to do it. That's motor planning. Here are 9 materials that help.

@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/occupational therapy / motor development / praxis / sensory-motor integration / developmental coordination/motor planning / praxis / ideation / sequencing / movement organization / dyspraxia support/9-materials-that-help-with-motor-planning#faq

mainEntity.name: Why does my child's body doesn't cooperate with their intentions. they can watch someone do something — kick a ball, jump over a rope, put on a jacket — and understand exactly what's supposed to happen. but when they try to do it themselves, it's like the instructions get lost somewhere between their brain and their muscles. new movements are the hardest. other kids see something once and their bodies just figure it out. my child has to consciously think through every step: first this foot, then this hand, now turn this way, now push here. by the time they've figured out the sequence, the moment has passed or they're so mentally exhausted they give up. getting dressed is a daily battle. each piece of clothing is a problem to solve — which hole does my arm go through, which way does this face, how do i get my head through here? things other kids do on autopilot require my child's full concentration. and then there's the clumsiness that comes with it. they bump into doorframes, misjudge when to duck, trip over things they saw perfectly well. it's not that they didn't see the obstacle — it's that they couldn't plan how to get their body around it in time. sports are heartbreaking to watch. they want so badly to keep up with their friends. they understand the games perfectly. but their bodies can't execute the movements fast enough or smoothly enough. they're always a beat behind, always looking awkward while trying their hardest. fine motor planning is just as hard. buttons, zippers, shoelaces, using scissors, writing — anything that requires their fingers to execute a sequence of movements is painstakingly slow. teachers think they're not trying or not paying attention, but the opposite is true — they're trying harder than any other kid in the class, they're just fighting their own motor system every step of the way. i've watched my child become self-conscious about their body. they avoid new physical activities because they know they'll struggle. they hang back when other kids are climbing and running because they're embarrassed about how hard it is for them. i need to understand what's happening and how to help them. there must be tools and strategies that can help their brain and body work together better?

acceptedAnswer.text: MOTOR PLANNING DEFICIT / DYSPRAXIA / PRAXIS DISORDER / DEVELOPMENTAL COORDINATION DISORDER (MOTOR PLANNING COMPONENT) / IDEOMOTOR DYSFUNCTION! Motor planning (praxis) is the neurological process of conceiving, organizing, sequencing, and executing unfamiliar motor actions. It involves three components: ideation (knowing what to do), motor planning (figuring out how to do it), and execution (carrying out the plan). Children with motor planning difficulties (dyspraxia) struggle primarily with the 'how' — they can perceive what movement is needed and have the physical strength and range of motion to perform it, but cannot efficiently organize the sequence of muscle activations required. This results in slow, effortful, awkward movement acquisition; difficulty with novel motor tasks; poor generalization of motor learning; and ongoing struggle with complex motor sequences. Motor planning deficits significantly impact daily living skills (dressing, feeding, grooming), academic performance (writing, cutting, manipulating materials), play and physical activity, and social participation. The underlying cause is typically neurological differences in how the brain processes sensory-motor information. Intervention focuses on providing rich sensory-motor experiences, breaking down complex movements into learnable steps, building motor memory through repetition, and using external supports to guide movement planning.

mainEntity.name: What is Motor Planning Deficit / Dyspraxia / Praxis Disorder / Developmental Coordination Disorder (Motor Planning Component) / Ideomotor Dysfunction?

acceptedAnswer.text: Motor planning (praxis) is the neurological process of conceiving, organizing, sequencing, and executing unfamiliar motor actions. It involves three components: ideation (knowing what to do), motor planning (figuring out how to do it), and execution (carrying out the plan). Children with motor planning difficulties (dyspraxia) struggle primarily with the 'how' — they can perceive what movement is needed and have the physical strength and range of motion to perform it, but cannot efficiently organize the sequence of muscle activations required. This results in slow, effortful, awkward movement acquisition; difficulty with novel motor tasks; poor generalization of motor learning; and ongoing struggle with complex motor sequences. Motor planning deficits significantly impact daily living skills (dressing, feeding, grooming), academic performance (writing, cutting, manipulating materials), play and physical activity, and social participation. The underlying cause is typically neurological differences in how the brain processes sensory-motor information. Intervention focuses on providing rich sensory-motor experiences, breaking down complex movements into learnable steps, building motor memory through repetition, and using external supports to guide movement planning. Common signs include: Difficulty learning new motor tasks despite adequate strength and understanding, Needs to watch and think through movements others do automatically, Clumsy, awkward movement patterns especially with novel activities, Slow, effortful performance of motor sequences (dressing, writing, sports), Poor body awareness — bumps into things, misjudges distances.

mainEntity.name: How does a Obstacle Course Components help with motor planning deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Obstacle courses are the gold standard for motor planning intervention. They require the child to look ahead, plan a sequence of movements, execute each section, and transition between different motor challenges. Unlike repetitive single-skill practice, obstacle courses demand flexible motor planning — the child must generate new motor plans for each obstacle and sequence them together. Course components (tunnels, balance beams, stepping stones, hurdles, crawl-unders) can be arranged in different configurations to prevent rote memorization and force ongoing planning. The child learns to read the environment, anticipate movement demands, and organize their body accordingly. Each successful course completion builds motor planning confidence and capacity. As the child improves, courses can become longer and more complex. Price: ₹1,000–8,000 (basic set to comprehensive kit). DIY option: Create obstacle courses using household items: couch cushions to climb over, chairs to crawl under, tape lines to walk on, pillows to jump between, tunnels made from blankets over chairs. The key is variety and reconfiguration — change the course regularly so the child must generate new motor plans rather than memorizing one route. Start simple (3-4 obstacles) and increase complexity as motor planning improves. Talk through the course first: 'First you'll crawl under, then step on these, then climb over.' This verbal planning supports the motor planning process. Celebrate completion and gradually reduce verbal scaffolding as the child's independent planning improves. Safety: Ensure course is age-appropriate and surfaces are safe. Supervise closely. Remove hazards around course area.

mainEntity.name: How does a Weighted Lap Pad / Vest help with motor planning deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Motor planning depends heavily on proprioceptive input — the sense of where your body is in space, how your joints are positioned, and how much force you're using. Children with motor planning difficulties often have poor proprioceptive processing, making it hard for their brains to know where their body parts are without looking. Weighted items (lap pads, vests, blankets) provide deep pressure input that enhances proprioceptive awareness. This calms the nervous system while simultaneously giving the brain better information about the body's position. With improved body awareness, motor planning becomes more accurate — the child has better 'data' about where their body is starting from when generating movement plans. Weighted items are especially helpful before motor planning activities. Price: ₹800–3,000. DIY option: Select weighted items appropriate for child's size — general guideline is 5-10% of body weight, but consult with OT for specific recommendations. Use weighted lap pad during seated activities or before motor planning tasks. Weighted vest can be worn during gross motor activities if appropriate and recommended by therapist. The deep pressure 'wakes up' the proprioceptive system, giving the brain better body position information. Some children benefit from wearing the weighted item; others prefer it used briefly before activities. Observe the child's response — they should appear calmer and more organized, not restricted or uncomfortable. Safety: Consult occupational therapist for weight recommendations. Do not use for extended periods without guidance. Watch for signs of discomfort. Not appropriate for all children.

mainEntity.name: How does a Therapy Ball (Large Exercise Ball) help with motor planning deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Large therapy balls challenge the vestibular and proprioceptive systems while requiring constant motor adjustment. Sitting on an unstable surface demands ongoing postural planning — the child must continuously make micro-adjustments to stay balanced. Bouncing, rolling over, and performing movements on the ball all require motor planning in a dynamic, unpredictable context. This is exactly the kind of practice motor planning systems need. The ball also provides heavy proprioceptive input through compression and bouncing. Activities on the ball can be graded from simple (just sitting) to complex (reaching for objects while bouncing, transitioning positions). The unpredictability of the ball surface forces the brain to generate and update motor plans constantly, building planning capacity. Price: ₹500–1,500. DIY option: Select ball size based on child's height — when seated, hips and knees should be at approximately 90 degrees. Activities progress from simple to complex: sitting and bouncing (basic vestibular/proprioceptive input), reaching for objects while seated (adding motor planning demands), lying prone over ball and walking hands out (upper body planning), transitioning on/off the ball in different ways, catching/throwing while seated on ball. The instability is the point — the brain must continuously plan and adjust. Always supervise ball activities closely. Can also use ball for wall push-ups (heavy work) or rolling under adult support for vestibular input. Safety: Always supervise therapy ball activities. Use appropriately sized ball. Ensure adequate space around ball. Spot the child during challenging activities.

mainEntity.name: How does a Visual Sequence Cards for Motor Tasks help with motor planning deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Children with motor planning difficulties struggle to organize the steps of a movement sequence in their minds. Visual sequence cards externalize this process — the steps are visible, ordered, and can be referenced throughout the task. For dressing, brushing teeth, handwriting strokes, craft activities, or any multi-step motor task, cards showing each step in order provide the organizational scaffold the child's brain can't generate independently. This isn't a crutch — it's training. With repeated practice using visual sequences, the child internalizes the motor plan. Eventually, they can perform the sequence without the visual support because their brain has learned the organization. Visual sequence cards also allow the child to self-monitor: 'Which step am I on? What comes next?' Price: ₹200–800 (or DIY). DIY option: Create visual sequence cards for challenging motor tasks: Take photos or draw simple pictures of each step of the task, arrange in clear order (numbered or left-to-right), and display where the child can reference during the task. For dressing: each piece of clothing shown in order with body position needed. For handwriting letters: stroke sequence shown step by step. For craft activities: each step photographed. Start with simple 3-4 step sequences and increase complexity. Initially, talk through each step as the child does it. Gradually fade verbal support while keeping visual support, then fade visual support as motor plan becomes automatic. Safety: Ensure cards are clear and visible at appropriate level. Update cards as child progresses.

mainEntity.name: How does a Resistive Putty / Therapy Dough help with motor planning deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Resistive putty provides heavy proprioceptive input to the hands while requiring motor planning for manipulation. Unlike passive squeezing, using putty for specific tasks (hiding and finding beads, forming shapes, pulling apart, rolling into balls) requires the child to plan and execute fine motor sequences against resistance. The resistance itself is therapeutic — it gives the brain strong feedback about hand position, finger placement, and force used, which supports motor planning. Putty activities bridge the gap between sensory input and motor output: the sensory feedback from the resistance helps the brain plan the next movement in the sequence. Different resistance levels allow progression as hand strength and motor planning improve. Price: ₹300–800. DIY option: Select putty resistance appropriate for child — softer for beginning, firmer as strength and skill develop. Structured activities, not just squeezing: Hide small objects (beads, coins) in putty and have child find them (requires planned finger movements). Roll putty into balls, snakes, shapes (motor sequencing). Pinch putty between specific fingers (targeted motor planning). Pull putty apart slowly (sustained effort with planned grip). Create putty 'pancakes' and use cookie cutters. The key is purposeful manipulation, not passive squeezing. Can also DIY dough with flour/salt/water recipes, adjusting consistency for resistance. Safety: Choose non-toxic putty. Ensure hidden objects are appropriate size (not choking hazards). Supervise use with young children.

mainEntity.name: How does a Scooter Board help with motor planning deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Scooter boards require bilateral coordination, motor sequencing, and body-in-space planning that challenges motor planning systems intensively. To propel the board, the child must coordinate arms or legs in a planned sequence while adjusting to the board's movement. This is motor planning in a vestibular-rich context. Prone (on tummy) scooter use requires motor planning for arm coordination and core stabilization. Seated scooter use requires leg coordination and balance maintenance. Activities like scooter races, collecting objects while scooting, and navigating scooter obstacle courses add layers of motor planning challenge. The proprioceptive input from pushing against the floor combined with vestibular input from movement provides rich sensory information to support motor planning. Price: ₹800–2,500. DIY option: Select sturdy scooter board with smooth casters. Start with prone position (lying on tummy): child propels using arms in coordinated pattern. Progress to seated: child propels using legs, maintaining balance. Activities add planning challenge: Scooter to collect objects scattered around room (requires planning route). Scooter through obstacle course (requires planning around obstacles). Scooter relay races (requires planning speed and direction). Scooter with pulling objects (adds resistance). Scooter down ramp (adds vestibular challenge). The combination of movement, coordination, and goal-directed activity challenges motor planning systems intensely. Safety: Use on smooth surfaces only. Clear area of obstacles. Supervise closely. Ensure appropriate weight capacity. Protect hands from casters.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Motor Planning Deficit
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Motor planning (praxis) is the neurological ability to conceive, organize, sequence, and execute unfamiliar motor actions. It involves three components: ideation (generating the idea of what to do), motor planning (figuring out how to do it), and execution (carrying out the movement plan). Children with motor planning difficulties struggle primarily with the planning component — translating the 'what' into the 'how.' Intervention focuses on enriching the sensory-motor foundation (proprioception, vestibular input) that supports planning, providing structured opportunities to practice generating and executing motor plans, and building automaticity through repetition so that planned movements become automatic motor patterns.

This results in slow, effortful, awkward movement acquisition; difficulty with novel motor tasks; poor generalization of motor learning; and ongoing struggle with complex motor sequences. Motor planning deficits significantly impact daily living skills (dressing, feeding, grooming), academic performance (writing, cutting, manipulating materials), play and physical activity, and social participation. The underlying cause is typically neurological differences in how the brain processes sensory-motor information. Intervention focuses on providing rich sensory-motor experiences, breaking down complex movements into learnable steps, building motor memory through repetition, and using external supports to guide movement planning. signOrSymptom.name: Difficulty learning new motor tasks despite adequate strength and understanding
Needs to watch and think through movements others do automatically
Clumsy, awkward movement patterns especially with novel activities
Slow, effortful performance of motor sequences (dressing, writing, sports)
Poor body awareness — bumps into things, misjudges distances
Struggles with bilateral coordination (using both sides of body together)
Avoids new physical activities or play equipment

possibleTreatment.name: Sensory Integration Therapy
possibleTreatment.description: Occupational therapy using graded sensory input

item.name: Obstacle Course Components

Unlike repetitive single-skill practice, obstacle courses demand flexible motor planning — the child must generate new motor plans for each obstacle and sequence them together. Course components (tunnels, balance beams, stepping stones, hurdles, crawl-unders) can be arranged in different configurations to prevent rote memorization and force ongoing planning. The child learns to read the environment, anticipate movement demands, and organize their body accordingly. Each successful course completion builds motor planning confidence and capacity. As the child improves, courses can become longer and more complex.

Children with motor planning difficulties often have poor proprioceptive processing, making it hard for their brains to know where their body parts are without looking. Weighted items (lap pads, vests, blankets) provide deep pressure input that enhances proprioceptive awareness. With improved body awareness, motor planning becomes more accurate — the child has better 'data' about where their body is starting from when generating movement plans. additionalProperty.value: Select weighted items appropriate for child's size — general guideline is 5-10% of body weight, but consult with OT for specific recommendations. Use weighted lap pad during seated activities or before motor planning tasks. Weighted vest can be worn during gross motor activities if appropriate and recommended by therapist. The deep pressure 'wakes up' the proprioceptive system, giving the brain better body position information. Some children benefit from wearing the weighted item; others prefer it used briefly before activities. Observe the child's response — they should appear calmer and more organized, not restricted or uncomfortable.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Consult occupational therapist for weight recommendations. Do not use for extended periods without guidance. Watch for signs of discomfort. Not appropriate for all children.

item.name: Therapy Ball (Large Exercise Ball)
item.description: Large therapy balls challenge the vestibular and proprioceptive systems while requiring constant motor adjustment. Sitting on an unstable surface demands ongoing postural planning — the child must continuously make micro-adjustments to stay balanced. Bouncing, rolling over, and performing movements on the ball all require motor planning in a dynamic, unpredictable context. This is exactly the kind of practice motor planning systems need. The ball also provides heavy proprioceptive input through compression and bouncing. Activities on the ball can be graded from simple (just sitting) to complex (reaching for objects while bouncing, transitioning positions). The unpredictability of the ball surface forces the brain to generate and update motor plans constantly, building planning capacity.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Select ball size based on child's height — when seated, hips and knees should be at approximately 90 degrees. Activities progress from simple to complex: sitting and bouncing (basic vestibular/proprioceptive input), reaching for objects while seated (adding motor planning demands), lying prone over ball and walking hands out (upper body planning), transitioning on/off the ball in different ways, catching/throwing while seated on ball. The instability is the point — the brain must continuously plan and adjust. Always supervise ball activities closely. Can also use ball for wall push-ups (heavy work) or rolling under adult support for vestibular input.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Always supervise therapy ball activities. Use appropriately sized ball. Ensure adequate space around ball. Spot the child during challenging activities.

item.name: Visual Sequence Cards for Motor Tasks
item.description: Children with motor planning difficulties struggle to organize the steps of a movement sequence in their minds. Visual sequence cards externalize this process — the steps are visible, ordered, and can be referenced throughout the task. For dressing, brushing teeth, handwriting strokes, craft activities, or any multi-step motor task, cards showing each step in order provide the organizational scaffold the child's brain can't generate independently. This isn't a crutch — it's training. With repeated practice using visual sequences, the child internalizes the motor plan. Eventually, they can perform the sequence without the visual support because their brain has learned the organization. Visual sequence cards also allow the child to self-monitor: 'Which step am I on? What comes next?'

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create visual sequence cards for challenging motor tasks: Take photos or draw simple pictures of each step of the task, arrange in clear order (numbered or left-to-right), and display where the child can reference during the task. For dressing: each piece of clothing shown in order with body position needed. For handwriting letters: stroke sequence shown step by step. For craft activities: each step photographed. Start with simple 3-4 step sequences and increase complexity. Initially, talk through each step as the child does it. Gradually fade verbal support while keeping visual support, then fade visual support as motor plan becomes automatic.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure cards are clear and visible at appropriate level. Update cards as child progresses.

item.name: Resistive Putty / Therapy Dough
item.description: Resistive putty provides heavy proprioceptive input to the hands while requiring motor planning for manipulation. Unlike passive squeezing, using putty for specific tasks (hiding and finding beads, forming shapes, pulling apart, rolling into balls) requires the child to plan and execute fine motor sequences against resistance. The resistance itself is therapeutic — it gives the brain strong feedback about hand position, finger placement, and force used, which supports motor planning. Putty activities bridge the gap between sensory input and motor output: the sensory feedback from the resistance helps the brain plan the next movement in the sequence. Different resistance levels allow progression as hand strength and motor planning improve.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Select putty resistance appropriate for child — softer for beginning, firmer as strength and skill develop. Structured activities, not just squeezing: Hide small objects (beads, coins) in putty and have child find them (requires planned finger movements). Roll putty into balls, snakes, shapes (motor sequencing). Pinch putty between specific fingers (targeted motor planning). Pull putty apart slowly (sustained effort with planned grip). Create putty 'pancakes' and use cookie cutters. The key is purposeful manipulation, not passive squeezing. Can also DIY dough with flour/salt/water recipes, adjusting consistency for resistance.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Choose non-toxic putty. Ensure hidden objects are appropriate size (not choking hazards). Supervise use with young children.

item.name: Scooter Board
item.description: Scooter boards require bilateral coordination, motor sequencing, and body-in-space planning that challenges motor planning systems intensively. To propel the board, the child must coordinate arms or legs in a planned sequence while adjusting to the board's movement. This is motor planning in a vestibular-rich context. Prone (on tummy) scooter use requires motor planning for arm coordination and core stabilization. Seated scooter use requires leg coordination and balance maintenance. Activities like scooter races, collecting objects while scooting, and navigating scooter obstacle courses add layers of motor planning challenge. The proprioceptive input from pushing against the floor combined with vestibular input from movement provides rich sensory information to support motor planning.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Select sturdy scooter board with smooth casters. Start with prone position (lying on tummy): child propels using arms in coordinated pattern. Progress to seated: child propels using legs, maintaining balance. Activities add planning challenge: Scooter to collect objects scattered around room (requires planning route). Scooter through obstacle course (requires planning around obstacles). Scooter relay races (requires planning speed and direction). Scooter with pulling objects (adds resistance). Scooter down ramp (adds vestibular challenge). The combination of movement, coordination, and goal-directed activity challenges motor planning systems intensely.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Use on smooth surfaces only. Clear area of obstacles. Supervise closely. Ensure appropriate weight capacity. Protect hands from casters.

item.name: Pegboard with Patterns
item.description: Pegboards combine visual motor planning with fine motor execution. The child must look at a pattern, plan which peg goes where, and execute the placement with precision. This requires ideation (what am I making?), motor planning (which hand movements will get the peg in the right hole?), and execution (actually placing the peg accurately). Unlike random peg play, pattern copying adds cognitive-motor integration — the child must translate visual information into motor plans. Pegboards also build hand strength and precision grasp patterns. Activities can progress from copying simple patterns (one color, basic shapes) to complex designs (multiple colors, detailed pictures). The structured, goal-directed nature of pattern copying ensures motor planning practice isn't random but purposeful.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Select pegboard with appropriately sized pegs for child's hand development. Create or print patterns for copying — start simple and progress: Single color row or column patterns. Simple shapes (square, triangle). Multi-color patterns. Complex designs and pictures. Child looks at the pattern, identifies next peg placement, and executes. Encourage verbal planning: 'I need a red peg here.' Building their own designs adds creative motor planning. Timed activities can add challenge for older children. Pegboards can also be positioned vertically (attached to wall) to add shoulder stability demands to the fine motor planning task.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure pegs are appropriate size for child (not choking hazards for young children). Supervise use with small pegs.

item.name: Balance Beam / Walking Rail
item.description: Balance beams require ongoing motor planning for postural adjustment, weight shifting, and foot placement. Unlike walking on flat ground (which becomes automatic), walking on a narrow surface demands conscious motor planning — each step must be planned and executed carefully. This controlled, slowed-down motor practice is ideal for children whose motor planning breaks down with speed. The balance beam allows them to plan each movement deliberately. Variations add planning complexity: walking forward, backward, sideways; stepping over obstacles on the beam; carrying objects while walking; walking with different foot patterns. The beam can be raised or lowered to grade difficulty. The concentrated practice of planned, controlled movement builds motor planning capacity that generalizes to faster, automatic movements.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Start with floor-level 'beams' (tape line on floor, flat board on floor) before raising to elevated beams. Width and height can be gradually decreased/increased as skill develops. Activities progress: Walk forward on wide beam. Walk forward on narrow beam. Walk backward. Walk sideways. Step over small obstacles placed on beam. Carry an object while walking. Walk with beanbag on head. Walk heel-to-toe. Change direction on beam. For home: use sturdy boards, pool noodles (half-rounds), or tape lines before purchasing actual balance equipment. The key is deliberate, planned walking rather than fast, automatic walking.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Supervise balance activities closely. Use mats around elevated beams. Start at floor level and progress gradually. Spot the child as needed.

item.name: Imitation Activity Cards
item.description: Motor imitation — copying observed movements — requires the child to observe a position or movement, generate a motor plan to replicate it, and execute that plan with their own body. This is motor planning in its purest form: translating visual input into motor output. Children with motor planning difficulties struggle with imitation because generating the 'how' is their core challenge. Structured imitation practice using activity cards builds this translation capacity. Cards showing body positions (yoga poses, funny positions, action poses) give the child a clear target to imitate. Starting with static poses and progressing to movement sequences builds imitation-based motor planning systematically. This skill generalizes to learning from demonstration in all contexts.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create or purchase cards showing body positions to imitate: yoga poses, animal movements, action poses, funny positions. Start simple: basic standing poses with arms in different positions. Progress to: complex poses involving whole body, balance poses, movement sequences (not just static positions), animal walks (bear walk, crab walk). Practice: child looks at card, plans how to get their body into that position, and executes. Provide feedback and help them adjust. Mirror work helps — child can see themselves while imitating. Games: pose matching, pose freeze dance, follow the leader with pose cards. Eventually, progress to imitating movements demonstrated by another person (live model).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure poses are developmentally appropriate. Provide support for balance poses. Clear space for movement.

socialMedia.caption: When the body knows but can't figure out how. Some children understand exactly what movement they want to make but can't get their body to execute the plan. That's motor planning — and it's trainable. Here are 9 materials that help: obstacle courses, weighted items, therapy balls, visual sequence cards, resistive putty, scooter boards, pegboards, balance beams, and imitation cards. Build the brain-body connection. 🔖 #MotorPlanning #Dyspraxia #OccupationalTherapy #PinnacleBlooms
